name: APEASE
description: APEASE entry and reference layer. Six criteria with BCIO ID mappings, high-level formulas, and trigger conditions. Activated only by explicit trigger — not default runtime mode. Deep analysis spec: APEASE_FULL_SPEC.json.
role_in_AISA: Entry gate for APEASE mode. Answers: should APEASE be activated? Which criterion maps to which BCIO IDs? APEASE is not active by default — requires explicit trigger E1/E2/E3.
iteration_logic: APEASE is not a one-time gate but an iterative navigation system. After each wave: re-measure all six criteria, identify which failed thresholds, redesign the intervention using the ontology, re-launch. The ontology provides the solution space; APEASE provides the coordinate system.

Acceptability.what_we_measure: Acceptability across ALL stakeholders: target group, funders, practitioners, community groups, commercial groups. Primary: % not refusing at first contact (target group). Also: practitioner survey + adherence, funder qualitative assessment, community position analysis.
Acceptability.formula: A = (N_total - N_refused) / N_total × 100% | A_norm = A / 100 | A_weighted = w_target × A_target + w_practitioners × A_practitioners + w_funders × A_funders | A_predicted = A_base_analog × context_adjustment_factor (0.7–1.3)
Acceptability.operability: Requires pilot contact or literature analogue. Before launch: expert judgement. After pilot: real measurement.
Acceptability.veto_logic: Define threshold before launch. If A < threshold: redesign first contact mode or source.

bcio_terms.label: healthcare access
bcio_terms.definition: A <behavioural opportunity> regarding how easy it is for a person to approach and use a healthcare service.

bcio_terms.label: belief about healthcare accessibility
bcio_terms.definition: A <belief about one's environment> in terms of how easy it is for a person to approach and use a healthcare service.

bcio_terms.label: belief about barriers to a behaviour
bcio_terms.definition: A <belief> about a factor that could restrict or impede a person from engaging in a behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: belief about the credibility of a message's source
bcio_terms.definition: A <belief about message> in terms of whether a verbal, graphic, written or recorded communication is provided by people or institutions who provide reliable information.

bcio_terms.label: belief about message trustworthiness
bcio_terms.definition: A <belief about message> in terms of whether a verbal, graphic, written or recording communication provides information that can be relied on as honest or truthful.

bcio_terms.label: motivation to comply
bcio_terms.definition: A <behavioural motivation> to act in accordance with the likely approval of others.

bcio_terms.label: approval-based motivation
bcio_terms.definition: A <need-based motivation> related to gaining or maintaining social approval or a positive self-evaluation.

bcio_terms.label: attitude
bcio_terms.definition: A <mental disposition> that is an affective attitude or an evaluative belief about something.

bcio_terms.label: normative belief
bcio_terms.definition: A <perceived norm> regarding whether key others think one should perform a behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: appraisal of social identity
bcio_terms.definition: An emotional-relevance appraisal> regarding one's social group memberships.

bcio_terms.label: motivational disposition not to change behaviour
bcio_terms.definition: A <motivational disposition> not to change one’s behaviour pattern.

bcio_terms.label: motivation to deviate
bcio_terms.definition: A <behavioural motivation> to act contrary to likely approval of others.

bcio_terms.label: need for autonomy
bcio_terms.definition: A <psychological need> to have a sense of personal agency and initiative.

bcio_terms.label: need for a sense of safety
bcio_terms.definition: A <psychological need> to feel safe from threats.

bcio_terms.label: belief about the positive health consequences of a behaviour
bcio_terms.definition: A <belief about health consequences of behaviour> in which the consequences are evaluated as positive.

bcio_terms.label: belief about susceptibility to a threat
bcio_terms.definition: A <belief> about vulnerability to a threat.

bcio_terms.label: belief about consequences of behaviour
bcio_terms.definition: A <belief about consequences of an occurrence> in terms of what results from or follows the performance of a behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: awareness
bcio_terms.definition: A <mental disposition> that is realized by experiencing events, objects, sensory patterns or cognitive representations.

bcio_terms.label: knowledge
bcio_terms.definition: A <mental disposition> to understand the nature of the world, or a specific aspect of the world, that corresponds to the actual state of the world and is acquired through experience or learning.

bcio_terms.label: leadership role
bcio_terms.definition: A social role in which the holder is given or creates authority to exert influence on other members of a social group.

bcio_terms.label: friend
bcio_terms.definition: A relatedness between person source and the target population that is a person whom the participant knows, likes and trusts, typically exclusive of sexual or family relations.

bcio_terms.label: patient communication style
bcio_terms.definition: A communication style conveying willingness to take time to understand or complete a process.

bcio_terms.label: autonomy-supportive communication style
bcio_terms.definition: A communication style which aims to promote the recipient's capacity to make their own decisions.

bcio_terms.label: present information from credible influence BCT
bcio_terms.definition: An <awareness of other people's thoughts, feelings and actions BCT> that presents information from a credible person or organisation to influence the behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: increase awareness of others approval BCT
bcio_terms.definition: An <awareness of other people's thoughts, feelings and actions BCT> that increases awareness of whether others will like, approve, dislike, or disapprove of the behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: mental plan for coping with barriers
bcio_terms.definition: A <mental plan for a behaviour> regarding a strategy to deal with conditions that could prevent the performance of a behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: willingness to change behaviour
bcio_terms.definition: A <bodily disposition> to change one's behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: willingness to adopt new behaviours
bcio_terms.definition: A <willingness to change behaviour> to adopt new behaviours.

bcio_terms.label: appraisal of obligation to act
bcio_terms.definition: An <emotional-relevance appraisal> that represents an evaluation of how much one is personally obliged to respond to an event or person in need.

bcio_terms.label: belief about message trustworthiness
bcio_terms.definition: A <belief about message> in terms of whether a verbal, graphic, written or recording communication provides information that can be relied on as honest or truthful.

theories.label: Diffusion of Innovations
theories.gap_pattern: Diffusion of Innovations absent: text does not report adopter categories or S-curve trajectory. Early majority vs laggard penetration uncharacterised. Reach plateaus after early adopters invisible.

theories.label: Threshold Model
theories.gap_pattern: Threshold Model absent: text does not report individual activation thresholds for adoption. Critical mass dynamics and cascade tipping point undetected. Population-level reach non-linear dynamics invisible.

theories.label: Two-Step Flow
theories.gap_pattern: Two-Step Flow absent: text does not identify opinion leaders as intermediate reach agents. Direct vs mediated reach pathways indistinguishable. Multiplier effect of key influencers unquantified.

theories.label: Selective Exposure Theory
theories.gap_pattern: Selective Exposure Theory absent: text does not report pre-existing attitudes of reached population. Self-selection into intervention by already-motivated individuals undetected. Reach bias invisible.

theories.label: RE-AIM Framework
theories.gap_pattern: RE-AIM absent: text does not report representativeness of reached sample. Efficacy-effectiveness gap due to biased reach undetectable. Public health impact of intervention underestimated.

theories.label: Health Belief Model
theories.gap_pattern: Health Belief Model absent: text does not report perceived susceptibility or severity as reach determinants. Who self-selects into intervention based on risk perception uncharacterised. Reach skewed toward high-risk perceivers.

theories.label: Uses and Gratifications
theories.gap_pattern: Uses and Gratifications absent: text does not report what needs participants sought to satisfy through intervention. Active audience selection creating reach bias uncharacterised.

theories.label: Agenda-Setting Theory
theories.gap_pattern: Agenda-Setting absent: text does not report media framing effects on intervention reach. Competing media narratives reducing salience of target behaviour undetected.

theories.label: Volitional Help Sheet Model
theories.author: Armitage
theories.gap_pattern: Volitional Help Sheet absent: text does not report barrier-specific implementation intention planning. Generic planning BCT misses individualised barrier-action linkage.

theories.label: Stress and Coping Theory
theories.author: Lazarus & Folkman
theories.gap_pattern: Stress and Coping absent: text does not report primary and secondary appraisal independently. Threat appraisal without coping appraisal produces emotional-focused response not problem-focused behaviour change.

Acceptability.original_question: How far is it acceptable to key stakeholders? Includes: target group, potential funders, practitioners delivering intervention, relevant community and commercial groups.

formulas_L99.multi_stakeholder: A_weighted = w_target × A_target + w_practitioners × A_practitioners + w_funders × A_funders (w_target typically 0.5-0.7)

Practicability.what_we_measure: Three levels: (1) Initial feasibility — can it be launched with setup resources? (2) Scalability — at planned scale? (3) Sustainability — without external support in routine conditions? Pilot with grant ≠ sustainable in routine.
Practicability.formula: P = N_feasible / N_total_steps × 100% | P_weighted = (N_fully + 0.5 × N_conditionally) / N_total × 100% | P_critical = N_critical_feasible / N_critical_steps × 100% | P_sustainable = P_weighted × sustainability_factor (1.0=fully; 0.7=moderate external support; 0.5=grant-dependent)
Practicability.operability: Requires written protocol and resource inventory. Calculable before launch.
Practicability.veto_logic: If P < threshold: identify infeasible steps, find hire/outsource/redesign solution before launch.

bcio_terms.label: psychological behavioural capability
bcio_terms.definition: A <behavioural capability> that involves psychological abilities.

bcio_terms.label: physical behavioural capability
bcio_terms.definition: A <behavioural capability> that involves sensory or musculoskeletal abilities.

bcio_terms.label: social skill
bcio_terms.definition: A <social behavioural capability> acquired through training or practice.

bcio_terms.label: task complexity
bcio_terms.definition: A process attribute that is the complexity of thought or behaviour required to achieve a goal.

bcio_terms.label: physical behavioural opportunity
bcio_terms.definition: A <behavioural opportunity> that involves time and parts of the environmental system that do not involve people or organisations.

bcio_terms.label: social behavioural opportunity
bcio_terms.definition: A <behavioural opportunity> that involves the social environmental system.

bcio_terms.label: healthcare access
bcio_terms.definition: A <behavioural opportunity> regarding how easy it is for a person to approach and use a healthcare service.

bcio_terms.label: belief about healthcare accessibility
bcio_terms.definition: A <belief about one's environment> in terms of how easy it is for a person to approach and use a healthcare service.

bcio_terms.label: plan enactment
bcio_terms.definition: A <bodily process> by which a person attempts to follow the steps in a plan.

bcio_terms.label: action planning
bcio_terms.definition: <Planning> that has as a part some goal that is specified in terms of some behaviour and a time or place where the behaviour is to be enacted.

bcio_terms.label: action planning BCT
bcio_terms.definition: A <goal directed BCT> that involves making a detailed plan for the performance of the behaviour, which must include at least one of context, frequency, duration or intensity.

bcio_terms.label: decision simplification
bcio_terms.definition: A <cognitive process> used prior to decision-making that reduces the perceived scope or complexity of available options.

bcio_terms.label: degree of organisational hierarchy
bcio_terms.definition: An <organisational attribute> that is the strength and depth of formal authority relations between members of an organisation.

bcio_terms.label: belief about accountability for the consequences of an action
bcio_terms.definition: A <belief about responsibility>  about whether one should be accountable for the outcomes of an event.

bcio_terms.label: active goal
bcio_terms.definition: A <goal> that is being pursued at any one point in time.

bcio_terms.label: extent of past behavioural achievement
bcio_terms.definition: A personal attribute that is the extent to which the person’s past behaviour has reached or exceeded a standard.

bcio_terms.label: self-efficacy belief for a behaviour and its associated outcomes
bcio_terms.definition: A <self-efficacy belief> to organise and execute a behaviour and achieve the outcomes associated with this behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: self-efficacy belief for a behaviour
bcio_terms.definition: A <self-efficacy belief> to organise and execute a behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: situational self-efficacy belief for a behaviour
bcio_terms.definition: A <self-efficacy belief for a behaviour> when a barrier or facilitator of the behaviour is encountered.

bcio_terms.label: motivational disposition for behavioural maintenance
bcio_terms.definition: A <motivational disposition> in which the person is motivated to maintain an established behavioural pattern.

bcio_terms.label: regulatory behaviour
bcio_terms.definition: An experience-related behaviour that involves monitoring and acting upon some parts of the person or their environment.

bcio_terms.label: monitoring BCT
bcio_terms.definition: A <behaviour change technique> that involves gathering or using information about performance.

bcio_terms.label: provide feedback on behaviour BCT
bcio_terms.definition: A <provide feedback BCT> that provides information about the person's previous performance of the behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: arranging a healthcare service appointment
bcio_terms.definition: Utilising healthcare that involves scheduling a time to interact with a person or organisation providing healthcare.

bcio_terms.label: structured communication style
bcio_terms.definition: A communication style involving the communication content having constituent pre-planned parts.

bcio_terms.label: temporal orientation to the present
bcio_terms.definition: A <temporal orientation> to focus more on present than future outcomes.

bcio_terms.label: goal setting BCT
bcio_terms.definition: A <goal directed BCT> that sets goals.

bcio_terms.label: behaviour change intervention schedule of delivery
bcio_terms.definition: A BCI attribute that involves its temporal organisation.

bcio_terms.label: behaviour change intervention dose
bcio_terms.definition: A BCI content attribute that is its amount.

bcio_terms.label: behaviour change intervention mode of delivery
bcio_terms.definition: An attribute of a BCI delivery that is the physical or informational medium through which a BCI is provided.

theories.label: Principal-Agent Theory
theories.gap_pattern: Principal-Agent Theory absent: text does not report accountability structure between designer and implementer. Competing incentives driving protocol deviation undetected. Fidelity loss attributed to context not agency.

theories.label: Information Asymmetry
theories.gap_pattern: Information Asymmetry absent: text does not report knowledge differential between implementer and participant. Strategic behaviour by either party undetected. Reported compliance may differ from actual compliance.

theories.label: Street-Level Bureaucracy
theories.gap_pattern: Street-Level Bureaucracy absent: text does not report frontline implementer adaptations to protocol. Discretionary modification of intervention at delivery point invisible — delivered intervention diverges from designed.

theories.label: Rule-Following
theories.gap_pattern: Rule-Following absent: text does not report norm clarity or perceived legitimacy of protocol rules. Compliance driven by rule acceptance vs enforcement uncharacterised. Voluntary vs coerced fidelity indistinguishable.

theories.label: Error Propagation
theories.gap_pattern: Error Propagation absent: text does not report error accumulation across implementation steps. Single-point fidelity measures miss cascading deviations. Compounded deviation from original protocol unquantified.

theories.label: Implementation Intentions
theories.gap_pattern: Implementation Intentions absent: text does not report specific if-then action plans. Gap between intention and behaviour in barrier situations undetected. Situational failure of volitional control invisible.

theories.label: Active Implementation Frameworks
theories.gap_pattern: Active Implementation Frameworks absent: text does not assess installation, initial implementation or full implementation stages. Fidelity measured at wrong stage of implementation lifecycle.

theories.label: Program Differentiation
theories.gap_pattern: Program Differentiation absent: text does not distinguish active components from inert delivery features. Cannot isolate which elements drive effect — replication package undefined.

theories.label: Normalisation Process Theory
theories.gap_pattern: Normalisation Process Theory absent: text does not report embedding of intervention in routine practice. Sustainability of implementation after trial end unassessable. Normalisation as fidelity outcome invisible.

theories.label: Institutional Theory
theories.gap_pattern: Institutional Theory absent: text does not report institutional pressures shaping implementation. Isomorphic forces (coercive, normative, mimetic) driving superficial rather than substantive compliance invisible.

Practicability.original_question: Can it be implemented at scale in the intended context, with available material and human resources? What would need to be done to ensure resources and personnel are in place, and IS THE INTERVENTION SUSTAINABLE?

three_levels.initial_feasibility: Can it be launched with setup resources?
three_levels.scalability: Can it be implemented at planned scale?
three_levels.sustainability: Can it be maintained without external support in routine conditions? (Critical: pilot with grant ≠ sustainable in routine)

Effectiveness.what_we_measure: Two components: (1) Reach — % of target group actually exposed. (2) Effect size among reached — behaviour/outcome change in participants. Population effect = Reach × Effect size. Study with d=0.80 at Reach=20% < study with d=0.40 at Reach=70% for population.
Effectiveness.formula: E_binary = outcome(T) >= theta → {1,0} | Cohen's d = (M_int - M_ctrl) / SD_pooled | ARR = R_control - R_intervention | NNT = 1/ARR | E_population = Reach × ARR × N_target | E_norm = min(d / d_max, 1)
Effectiveness.operability: Before launch: forecast from meta-analyses and analogues. After launch: real measurement at T.
Effectiveness.veto_logic: If predicted effect size below theta: pilot on N=30 before full deployment.

bcio_terms.label: behaviour change intervention outcome behaviour
bcio_terms.definition: Human behaviour that is an intervention outcome.

bcio_terms.label: individual human behaviour change
bcio_terms.definition: A process that results in a difference in enactment of some individual human behaviour or individual human behaviour pattern from what would have been the case otherwise.

bcio_terms.label: behaviour change intervention mechanism of action
bcio_terms.definition: A process that is causally active in the relationship between a BCI scenario and its outcome behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: mechanism of action through feedback process to a person
bcio_terms.definition: A <behaviour change intervention mechanism of action> in which the causal influence occurs in a feedback process to a person.

bcio_terms.label: comparison of current behaviour with a standard
bcio_terms.definition: <Comparison of current situation with a standard> where the focus is on current behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: self-regulation through behavioural feedback
bcio_terms.definition: A <self-regulation of behaviour> that is informed by feedback.

bcio_terms.label: intrinsic motivation
bcio_terms.definition: <Autonomous motivation> due to a behaviour's inherent enjoyment, satisfaction or benefits to oneself.

bcio_terms.label: extrinsic motivation
bcio_terms.definition: <Behaviour motivation> that is based on external rewards, punishments or pressure regarding a behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: self-efficacy belief for a behaviour
bcio_terms.definition: A <self-efficacy belief> to organise and execute a behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: behaviour maintenance self-efficacy
bcio_terms.definition: A <self-efficacy belief for a behaviour> that continues a behaviour pattern.

bcio_terms.label: behavioural recovery self-efficacy
bcio_terms.definition: A <self-efficacy belief for a behaviour> following a lapse in that behaviour

bcio_terms.label: operant conditioning
bcio_terms.definition: <Associative learning> in which the likelihood of a particular behavioural response is influenced by the past consequences associated with performing that behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: associative learning
bcio_terms.definition: <Learning> that involves changing the strength of association between two or more mental processes.

bcio_terms.label: habit learning
bcio_terms.definition: <Associative learning> that involves strengthening an association between a stimulus and an impulse through repetition of co-occurrence of the stimulus and the behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: habitual behaviour
bcio_terms.definition: An <individual human behaviour> that results from a learnt stimulus-behaviour co-occurrence.

bcio_terms.label: generalisation in learning
bcio_terms.definition: <Associative learning> in which prior learning that an antecedent stimulus signals certain consequences of a behaviour spreads to a stimulus or behaviour that differs from the original stimulus or behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: learning
bcio_terms.definition: A <mental process> in which a lasting mental or behavioural change occurs as the result of experience.

bcio_terms.label: participant engagement with behaviour change intervention
bcio_terms.definition: Participant engagement with intervention, where the intervention focuses on changing behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: motivation to deviate
bcio_terms.definition: A <behavioural motivation> to act contrary to likely approval of others.

bcio_terms.label: belief about gain
bcio_terms.definition: A <belief> about the existence of an increase from an initial reference point.

bcio_terms.label: belief about reduction
bcio_terms.definition: A <belief> about the existence of a decrease from an initial reference point.

bcio_terms.label: belief about consequences of behaviour
bcio_terms.definition: A <belief about consequences of an occurrence> in terms of what results from or follows the performance of a behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: behaviour change intervention evaluation study risk of bias or error
bcio_terms.definition: An information content entity that is about the likelihood of the BCI evaluation finding misrepresenting the outcome behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: behaviour change intervention evaluation study
bcio_terms.definition: An intervention evaluation study of a BCI scenario.

bcio_terms.label: behaviour change intervention evaluation study plan
bcio_terms.definition: A plan for a BCI evaluation study.

bcio_terms.label: research study sample
bcio_terms.definition: A population whose behaviour is studied in a research study.

bcio_terms.label: monitoring BCT
bcio_terms.definition: A <behaviour change technique> that involves gathering or using information about performance.

bcio_terms.label: belief about the discrepancy between one?s current and target state
bcio_terms.definition: A <belief> about the discrepancy between one’s current and target state.

bcio_terms.label: attitude
bcio_terms.definition: A <mental disposition> that is an affective attitude or an evaluative belief about something.

bcio_terms.label: behavioural intention
bcio_terms.definition: A <mental disposition> to commit to enact or not enact a behaviour.

theories.label: Self-Determination Theory
theories.gap_pattern: Self-Determination Theory absent: text does not report autonomy, competence or relatedness satisfaction. Motivational quality (controlled vs autonomous) invisible — sustained engagement vs compliance indistinguishable.

theories.label: ABC Model / REBT
theories.gap_pattern: ABC Model / REBT absent: text does not report irrational beliefs mediating between activating event and consequence. B-layer (belief filter) uncharacterised — emotional and behavioural consequences appear stimulus-driven.

theories.label: Prospect Theory
theories.gap_pattern: Prospect Theory absent: text does not report loss vs gain framing of incentives or outcomes. Reference point shifts undetected. Loss aversion as driver of engagement or avoidance invisible.

theories.label: Flow Theory
theories.gap_pattern: Flow Theory absent: text does not report challenge-skill balance or absorption state. Engagement quality (flow vs anxiety vs boredom) uncharacterised. Intervention dose calibrated to wrong activation zone.

theories.label: Universal Law of Generalization
theories.gap_pattern: Universal Law of Generalisation absent: text does not assess transfer of learned behaviour to new contexts. Generalisation gradient from training context to target context unquantified — ecological validity gap.

theories.label: Social Learning Theory
theories.gap_pattern: Social Learning Theory absent: text does not report modelling, vicarious reinforcement or observational pathways. Social transmission of behaviour undetected. Peer effects on engagement invisible.

theories.label: Nudge / Choice Architecture
theories.gap_pattern: Nudge / Choice Architecture absent: text does not report default option structure or choice environment design. Engagement through automatic rather than deliberate pathways uncharacterised.

theories.label: Psychological Reactance Theory
theories.gap_pattern: Psychological Reactance absent: text does not report perceived autonomy threat. Backfire engagement (increased resistance) indistinguishable from non-response. Reactance as mechanism for null result invisible.

theories.label: Self-Efficacy Theory
theories.gap_pattern: Self-Efficacy Theory absent: text does not report self-efficacy beliefs for target behaviour. Confidence as prerequisite for engagement unassessed — low self-efficacy as barrier remains undetected.

theories.label: Operant Conditioning
theories.gap_pattern: Operant Conditioning absent: text does not report reinforcement schedule type or contingency structure. Engagement as function of reinforcement pattern uncharacterised — extinction risk after programme end unassessed.

Effectiveness.original_question: How effective is it (likely to be) in achieving policy objectives? How far will it REACH the intended target group and how large an effect will it have on those who are reached?

two_components.reach: % of target group actually reached by the intervention
two_components.effect_size_among_reached: How much behaviour/outcome changes in those who participated
two_components.population_effect: Reach × Effect size among reached = key metric for policies

outcome_specificity.operational: reduction in PHQ-9 by ≥5 points at 12 weeks, verified by repeat measurement

formulas_L99.normalised: E_norm = min(d / d_max, 1) where d_max set by context (typically 1.5–2.0)

Affordability.what_we_measure: Three levels: (1) Cost_inaction — what is currently spent per person due to the problem. (2) Cost_intervention — direct + indirect + setup costs. (3) Saving_projected — Cost_inaction × effect_size_reduction. Af_net = Cost_inaction − Cost_intervention. ROI = (Saving − Cost) / Cost × 100%.
Affordability.formula: Af_total = (C_direct + C_indirect) × N + C_setup | Af_net = Cost_inaction - Af_total | ROI = (Saving_projected - Af_total) / Af_total × 100% | Af_norm = 1 - min(Af_total / Budget_ceiling, 1) | Cost_inaction(T) = Cost_inaction_year1 × Σ(1+g)^t
Affordability.operability: Calculable from budget and operational plan before launch. Pure arithmetic.
Affordability.veto_logic: If Af_norm < threshold: redesign mode of delivery, source, or dose. Scale economy analysis required.

bcio_terms.label: behaviour change intervention dose
bcio_terms.definition: A BCI content attribute that is its amount.

bcio_terms.label: behaviour change intervention schedule of delivery
bcio_terms.definition: A BCI attribute that involves its temporal organisation.

bcio_terms.label: behaviour change intervention mode of delivery
bcio_terms.definition: An attribute of a BCI delivery that is the physical or informational medium through which a BCI is provided.

bcio_terms.label: face to face mode of delivery
bcio_terms.definition: Human interactional mode of delivery that involves an intervention source and recipient being together in the same location and communicating directly.

bcio_terms.label: electronic mode of delivery
bcio_terms.definition: Informational mode of delivery that involves electronic technology in the presentation of information to an intervention recipient.

bcio_terms.label: mass media mode of delivery
bcio_terms.definition: Informational mode of delivery of radio broadcast, television, online press and printed press to a mass audience.

bcio_terms.label: behaviour change intervention physical setting
bcio_terms.definition: A physical environment in which a BCI is delivered.

bcio_terms.label: behaviour change intervention source
bcio_terms.definition: A role played by a person, population or organisation that provides a BCI.

bcio_terms.label: behaviour change intervention study investigator
bcio_terms.definition: A role played by a person that contributes substantively to production or reporting of a BCI evaluation study.

bcio_terms.label: degree of organisational hierarchy
bcio_terms.definition: An <organisational attribute> that is the strength and depth of formal authority relations between members of an organisation.

bcio_terms.label: task complexity
bcio_terms.definition: A process attribute that is the complexity of thought or behaviour required to achieve a goal.

bcio_terms.label: research study sample
bcio_terms.definition: A population whose behaviour is studied in a research study.

bcio_terms.label: number of people in a population
bcio_terms.definition: A data item about the number of people in a population.

bcio_terms.label: arranging a healthcare service appointment
bcio_terms.definition: Utilising healthcare that involves scheduling a time to interact with a person or organisation providing healthcare.

bcio_terms.label: physical behavioural opportunity
bcio_terms.definition: A <behavioural opportunity> that involves time and parts of the environmental system that do not involve people or organisations.

bcio_terms.label: structured communication style
bcio_terms.definition: A communication style involving the communication content having constituent pre-planned parts.

bcio_terms.label: action planning BCT
bcio_terms.definition: A <goal directed BCT> that involves making a detailed plan for the performance of the behaviour, which must include at least one of context, frequency, duration or intensity.

bcio_terms.label: context-specific repetition of behaviour BCT
bcio_terms.definition: An <advise specific behaviour BCT> that advises the person to repeat the behaviour in the same context.

bcio_terms.label: gamification mode of delivery
bcio_terms.definition: Mode of delivery that involves application of typical elements of game playing  to other areas of activity, typically as an online marketing technique to encourage engagement with a product or service.

bcio_terms.label: intermittent schedule of reinforcement
bcio_terms.definition: A <schedule of reinforcement> in which the event following the behaviour occurs on some but not all occasions.

bcio_terms.label: leadership role
bcio_terms.definition: A social role in which the holder is given or creates authority to exert influence on other members of a social group.

bcio_terms.label: arrange support BCT
bcio_terms.definition: A <social support BCT> that organises support from another for the person.

bcio_terms.label: social learning
bcio_terms.definition: <Learning> that involves a social influence process.

bcio_terms.label: personal history part of intervention exposure
bcio_terms.definition: A personal history part  that includes exposure to one or more interventions.

bcio_terms.label: demonstrate the behaviour BCT
bcio_terms.definition: A <guide how to perform behaviour BCT> that provides an observable sample of the performance of the behaviour for the person to aspire to or imitate.

bcio_terms.label: goal setting BCT
bcio_terms.definition: A <goal directed BCT> that sets goals.

bcio_terms.label: monitoring BCT
bcio_terms.definition: A <behaviour change technique> that involves gathering or using information about performance.

bcio_terms.label: temporal orientation to the present
bcio_terms.definition: A <temporal orientation> to focus more on present than future outcomes.

bcio_terms.label: decision simplification
bcio_terms.definition: A <cognitive process> used prior to decision-making that reduces the perceived scope or complexity of available options.

bcio_terms.label: active goal
bcio_terms.definition: A <goal> that is being pursued at any one point in time.

theories.label: Mere Exposure Effect
theories.gap_pattern: Mere Exposure Effect absent: text does not report exposure frequency or repetition count. Dose module cannot distinguish novelty from familiarity effects. Engagement through repeated exposure is undetectable.

theories.label: Diminishing Marginal Returns
theories.gap_pattern: Diminishing Marginal Returns absent: text does not report dose-response curve shape. Assumption of linear dose-effect relationship persists unchallenged. Optimal dose threshold not identifiable.

theories.label: Cognitive Load Theory
theories.gap_pattern: Cognitive Load Theory absent: text does not report information density or working memory demands of intervention materials. Dose of cognitive complexity unquantified — overload risk invisible.

theories.label: Yerkes-Dodson Law
theories.gap_pattern: Yerkes-Dodson Law absent: text does not report arousal level of participants. Optimal activation zone for performance not assessed. Under- or over-stimulation indistinguishable from null effect.

theories.label: Dose-Response Relationship
theories.gap_pattern: Dose-Response Relationship absent: no quantitative dose-outcome mapping reported. Cannot assess whether effect is threshold, linear or saturation type. Dose optimisation impossible without this data.

theories.label: Weber-Fechner Law
theories.gap_pattern: Weber-Fechner Law absent: text does not report perceptual sensitivity to incremental dose changes. Subjective magnitude of dose change uncharacterised — same objective dose change may be imperceptible.

theories.label: Habituation/Sensitization
theories.gap_pattern: Habituation/Sensitization absent: text does not report change in response across repeated exposures. Cannot distinguish adaptation from genuine behaviour change. Tolerance to intervention dose invisible.

theories.label: Priming Effect
theories.gap_pattern: Priming Effect absent: text does not report prior stimulus context or associative pre-exposure. Subliminal dose of framing unaccounted for. Cannot detect how antecedent stimuli shaped measured response.

theories.label: Allostatic Load Model
theories.gap_pattern: Allostatic Load absent: text does not report cumulative stress burden on participants. Chronic overload as explanation for non-response or dropout undetectable.

theories.label: Hormesis
theories.gap_pattern: Hormesis absent: text does not test low-dose stimulation vs high-dose inhibition. U-shaped or inverted-U dose-response not considered. Potentially beneficial low-dose effects classified as null.

Affordability.original_question: How far can it be afforded when delivered at the scale intended? Can the necessary budget be found? Will it provide a good return on investment?

level_1_cost_of_inaction.what: What is currently spent per person in the target population due to the problem.

components.medical: Hospitalisations, outpatient, drugs, diagnostics, emergency, rehabilitation
components.productivity: Absenteeism (days absent) + presenteeism (productivity loss while present) + early disability + premature death
components.social_transfers: Disability benefits, housing subsidies, communal subsidies
components.legal: Law enforcement, judicial costs — applicable for addictions, aggressive behaviour, DUI
components.educational: Grade repetition, dropout, academic underperformance — for child/youth problems
components.burden_of_disease: DALY/QALY: YLD (years lived with disability) + YLL (years of life lost)
components.population_economic: Employment, consumption, communal expenditure, demand/supply levels — for large-scale social problems

level_2_cost_of_intervention.direct_per_participant: Specialist time (hours × rate × overhead), materials, premises rental, transport, IT per user, participant costs (transport, time, income loss)
level_2_cost_of_intervention.indirect_overhead: Staff training (including time), supervision, quality control, administration, monitoring and evaluation
level_2_cost_of_intervention.setup_costs: Protocol and materials development, IT infrastructure (one-time), initial staff training, piloting, ethics and regulatory approval

formulas_L99.cost_inaction_full: Cost_inaction = (C_medical + C_productivity + C_social + C_legal + C_educational) × N × T_horizon
formulas_L99.inaction_temporal: Cost_inaction(T) = Cost_inaction_year1 × Σ(1+g)^t for t=0..T-1 where g = annual growth rate (typically 5-15% for chronic conditions)

Affordability.closed_loop: Inaction generates accumulating Spill-over AE (mortality, chronification, social degradation, problem transmission) → each AE has monetary cost → total = Cost_inaction → Cost_inaction grows non-linearly with chronification → Af_net = Cost_inaction - Af_total → justifies intervention

Side_effects.what_we_measure: Four quadrants: (1) Adverse AE of intervention. (2) Beneficial spillover of intervention (halo, social contagion, family). (3) Adverse AE of inaction (chronification, mortality, social degradation). (4) Beneficial spillover of inaction (rare: antagonistic intervention effect). All four quadrants required. Ignoring positive spillover = systematic underestimation.
Side_effects.formula: S_adverse_int = Σ P(AE_i) × severity(AE_i) | S_weighted = Σ P(AE_i) × severity(AE_i) × reversibility_weight | S_beneficial_int = Σ P(BE_j) × magnitude(BE_j) | S_adverse_inaction = Σ P(AE_k) × severity(AE_k) | S_net = (S_adverse_inaction - S_adverse_intervention) + S_beneficial_intervention | S_norm = 1 - S_adverse_intervention / S_max | S_norm_adjusted = min(S_norm + S_beneficial_int / S_max × 0.2, 1.0)
Side_effects.operability: Requires Delphi, ethics review, or literature review to catalogue adverse effects. Before launch: expert judgement.
Side_effects.veto_logic: If S_norm < threshold: redesign to remove high-severity pathways. Mandatory stopping rules.

bcio_terms.label: behaviour change intervention evaluation study risk of bias or error
bcio_terms.definition: An information content entity that is about the likelihood of the BCI evaluation finding misrepresenting the outcome behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: motivation to deviate
bcio_terms.definition: A <behavioural motivation> to act contrary to likely approval of others.

bcio_terms.label: motivational disposition to change behaviour
bcio_terms.definition: A <motivational disposition> to change one’s behaviour pattern.

bcio_terms.label: motivational disposition not to change behaviour
bcio_terms.definition: A <motivational disposition> not to change one’s behaviour pattern.

bcio_terms.label: need for comfort
bcio_terms.definition: A <psychological need> to feel comfortable.

bcio_terms.label: need to hold a world view
bcio_terms.definition: A <psychological need> to hold a set of beliefs about the wider social environment.

bcio_terms.label: belief about reduction
bcio_terms.definition: A <belief> about the existence of a decrease from an initial reference point.

bcio_terms.label: belief about susceptibility to a threat
bcio_terms.definition: A <belief> about vulnerability to a threat.

bcio_terms.label: appraisal of social identity
bcio_terms.definition: An emotional-relevance appraisal> regarding one's social group memberships.

bcio_terms.label: social behavioural opportunity
bcio_terms.definition: A <behavioural opportunity> that involves the social environmental system.

bcio_terms.label: social environmental system
bcio_terms.definition: An <environmental system> that consists of people, their pets, social and cultural institutions and processes that influence the life of people and social groups.

bcio_terms.label: belief about accountability for the consequences of an action
bcio_terms.definition: A <belief about responsibility>  about whether one should be accountable for the outcomes of an event.

bcio_terms.label: degree of organisational hierarchy
bcio_terms.definition: An <organisational attribute> that is the strength and depth of formal authority relations between members of an organisation.

bcio_terms.label: drug withdrawal syndrome
bcio_terms.definition: A bodily disposition to undergo pathological processes resulting from cessation of a substance.

bcio_terms.label: subjective craving
bcio_terms.definition: A subjective affective feeling involving a strong desire or urge.

bcio_terms.label: arrange satiation BCT
bcio_terms.definition: An <expose to stimulus BCT> that provides sustained exposure to a stimulus to reduce or extinguish an unwanted behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: disengagement due to workload
bcio_terms.definition: A <mental disposition> to be detached from other people due to exhaustion experienced in one's working environment.

bcio_terms.label: task complexity
bcio_terms.definition: A process attribute that is the complexity of thought or behaviour required to achieve a goal.

bcio_terms.label: feeling an urge
bcio_terms.definition: A <subjective affective feeling> that  arises when an impulse to enact a behaviour is activated but not immediately acted upon.

bcio_terms.label: vicarious punishment BCT
bcio_terms.definition: An <increase awareness of consequences BCT> that prompts observation of another person being punished when they perform the behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: promise aversive consequence for behaviour BCT
bcio_terms.definition: A <promise consequence for behaviour BCT> where the consequence is aversive.

bcio_terms.label: promise aversive consequence for outcome of behaviour BCT
bcio_terms.definition: A <promise consequence for outcome of behaviour BCT> where the consequence is aversive.

bcio_terms.label: prompt social comparison BCT
bcio_terms.definition: An <awareness of other people's thoughts, feelings and actions BCT> that draws attention to other people's behaviour and compares it with the person's own behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: negative reinforcement process
bcio_terms.definition: A <reinforcement process> in which the likelihood of behaviour is increased it being followed by termination or avoidance of a negatively appraised behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: friendship network
bcio_terms.definition: A <social environmental system> that consists of persons who share a bond of mutual affection and are in contact or communication.

bcio_terms.label: low self-efficacy
bcio_terms.definition: An <personal attribute> of self-efficacy belief that is low.

bcio_terms.label: need for self-esteem
bcio_terms.definition: A <psychological need> to have a positive evaluation of oneself and one's qualities and characteristics.

bcio_terms.label: need for a sense of safety
bcio_terms.definition: A <psychological need> to feel safe from threats.

bcio_terms.label: antisocial behaviour
bcio_terms.definition: A socially-related behaviour that a population judges to be is contrary to the laws or accepted current norms of social conduct within a specific social context and causes annoyance and or disapproval in others.

bcio_terms.label: belief about consequences of behaviour
bcio_terms.definition: A <belief about consequences of an occurrence> in terms of what results from or follows the performance of a behaviour.

theories.label: Psychological Reactance Theory
theories.gap_pattern: Psychological Reactance absent: text does not report perceived autonomy threat. Backfire engagement (increased resistance) indistinguishable from non-response. Reactance as mechanism for null result invisible.

theories.label: Boomerang Effect
theories.author: Brehm
theories.gap_pattern: Intervention activates reactance (050947) instead of compliance: engagement reverses. Autonomy threat triggers motivation to deviate.

theories.label: Sleeper Effect
theories.author: Hovland & Weiss
theories.gap_pattern: Sleeper Effect absent: delayed persuasion from initially discounted source invisible. Short-term outcome measurement misses later effect emergence as source cue dissipates.

theories.label: Bystander Effect
theories.author: Darley & Latane
theories.gap_pattern: Accountability (050963) diffuses with group size: individual responsibility felt by none.

theories.label: Spotlight Effect
theories.author: Gilovich et al.
theories.gap_pattern: Spotlight Effect absent: self-consciousness about performance in intervention context inflating social desirability bias invisible.

theories.label: Catch-22
theories.gap_pattern: Rule excludes possibility of using exception — autonomy and accountability loop

theories.label: Abilene Paradox
theories.gap_pattern: Group collectively acts against individual preferences — social reach distorts individual engagement

theories.label: Bandwagon Effect
theories.gap_pattern: Adoption accelerates with perceived social reach — engagement driven by social proof

theories.label: Rational Addiction Model
theories.author: Becker & Murphy
theories.gap_pattern: Rational Addiction Model absent: text does not report intertemporal utility calculation. Rational tolerance and withdrawal anticipation as addiction maintenance mechanism invisible.

theories.label: Reflective-Impulsive Model
theories.author: Strack & Deutsch
theories.gap_pattern: Reflective-Impulsive Model absent: text does not distinguish automatic from reflective behaviour determinants. Intervention targets reflective system while behaviour driven by impulsive system.

Side_effects.critical_note: Original term is Spill-over, not Side-effects. Spill-over includes BOTH adverse and beneficial effects. Ignoring positive spillover = systematic underestimation of intervention value.

categories.physical: Adverse drug reactions, physical exhaustion from intensive training
categories.psychological: Anxiety in exposure therapy, therapist dependency, iatrogenic stigma, J-curve deterioration
categories.social: Stigmatisation of participation, family conflicts when disclosing problems, social isolation
categories.systemic: Resource displacement from other programmes, opportunity cost, unwanted policy effects
categories.economic: Participant costs (time, transport), income loss during participation
categories.displacement: Substitution of one unwanted behaviour with another (quit smoking → overeating)

categories.halo_effect: Programme on PA → improved nutrition + sleep without separate intervention (general self-efficacy increase)
categories.social_spread: Participant changes behaviour → changes norm in group → others change (social contagion)
categories.family_spillover: Intervention with one parent → behaviour change in children and partner (modelling + environment change)
categories.economic_spillover: Reduced AUD → increased motivation and productivity (halo)
categories.system_positive: Freeing healthcare resources from treating consequences → available for others
categories.normative_shift: Sufficient participants → changes descriptive norm in population (critical mass)

categories.social_degradation: Family breakdown, job loss, homelessness, criminalisation
categories.problem_transmission: Parental addiction → children; infectious diseases → community
categories.system_overload: Growing emergency service demands, rising late-stage treatment costs
categories.normative_consolidation: Unwanted behaviour normalises in group, becomes harder to change

Q4_beneficial_inaction.label: Positive spillover of inaction (rare, but exists)
Q4_beneficial_inaction.note: Antagonistic effect of intervention: coercive intervention → increased resistance → worse than without it. Stigmatising intervention → stigma amplifies problem.
Q4_beneficial_inaction.relevance: Important when evaluating Coercion and some forms of Restriction

formulas_L99.net_balance: S_net = (S_adverse_inaction - S_adverse_intervention) + S_beneficial_intervention (positive = intervention improves overall effect profile)
formulas_L99.norm_basic: S_norm = 1 - S_adverse_intervention / S_max where S_max = N_AE × 5
formulas_L99.monetise_inaction: Cost_spillover_inaction = Σ P(AE_k) × unit_cost(AE_k) × N → feeds back into Cost_inaction
formulas_L99.monetise_beneficial: Value_beneficial_spillover = Σ P(BE_j) × unit_value(BE_j) × N → adds to Saving_projected

stopping_rules.immediate_stop: Any severity=5 AE: ≥1 case. Severity=4 AE: ≥5% participants. Severity=3 AE escalating: ≥20% participants.
stopping_rules.planned_monitoring: Weekly AE screening (3-question form). Monthly structured team review. Positive spillover assessment: T=3mo and T=end_of_wave.

Equity.what_we_measure: Whether intervention increases OR decreases inequality. Tudor Hart Inverse Care Law: availability inversely proportional to need. Metrics: simple gap (Eq_gap), Slope Index of Inequality (SII), Concentration Index (CI), Burden-Reach Mismatch, Direction check. Direction check mandatory: Delta_inequality = Eq_gap_after - Eq_gap_before.
Equity.formula: Eq_gap = Reach_max - Reach_min [pp] | Eq_norm = 1 - Eq_gap / 100 | Mismatch_i = Burden_share_i - Reach_share_i | SII: Reach = alpha + beta × SES_rank (negative beta = lower SES → lower Reach) | CI = 2 × cov(Reach_i, SES_rank_i) / mean(Reach) | Delta_inequality = Eq_gap_after - Eq_gap_before (negative = reduced inequality)
Equity.operability: Requires population data and access geography. Often available in administrative databases.
Equity.veto_logic: If Eq_norm < threshold: redesign access points, add equity measures (subsidies, translators, outreach).

bcio_terms.label: healthcare access
bcio_terms.definition: A <behavioural opportunity> regarding how easy it is for a person to approach and use a healthcare service.

bcio_terms.label: belief about healthcare accessibility
bcio_terms.definition: A <belief about one's environment> in terms of how easy it is for a person to approach and use a healthcare service.

bcio_terms.label: research study sample
bcio_terms.definition: A population whose behaviour is studied in a research study.

bcio_terms.label: number of people in a population
bcio_terms.definition: A data item about the number of people in a population.

bcio_terms.label: behaviour change intervention population
bcio_terms.definition: An intervention population who are exposed to a behaviour change intervention.

bcio_terms.label: household income
bcio_terms.definition: An aggregate of the individual incomes received by all the members of a household.

bcio_terms.label: socioeconomic status category
bcio_terms.definition: A  data item about a category assigned to a person based on an assessment of a score calculated from various measures of their income, education, occupation, family size or household.

bcio_terms.label: social behavioural opportunity
bcio_terms.definition: A <behavioural opportunity> that involves the social environmental system.

bcio_terms.label: social environmental system
bcio_terms.definition: An <environmental system> that consists of people, their pets, social and cultural institutions and processes that influence the life of people and social groups.

bcio_terms.label: friendship network
bcio_terms.definition: A <social environmental system> that consists of persons who share a bond of mutual affection and are in contact or communication.

bcio_terms.label: leadership role
bcio_terms.definition: A social role in which the holder is given or creates authority to exert influence on other members of a social group.

bcio_terms.label: social learning
bcio_terms.definition: <Learning> that involves a social influence process.

bcio_terms.label: mass media mode of delivery
bcio_terms.definition: Informational mode of delivery of radio broadcast, television, online press and printed press to a mass audience.

bcio_terms.label: diffusion of innovation
bcio_terms.definition: The broad dissemination of new technologies, ideas, or practices through a population.

bcio_terms.label: social organisation behaviour
bcio_terms.definition: A socially-related behaviour that involves a person contributing to the functioning of a social structure or a person in relation to a social structure.

bcio_terms.label: intention for collective behaviour
bcio_terms.definition: A <behavioural intention> to enact a behaviour with one or more others.

bcio_terms.label: appraisal of social identity
bcio_terms.definition: An emotional-relevance appraisal> regarding one's social group memberships.

bcio_terms.label: normative belief
bcio_terms.definition: A <perceived norm> regarding whether key others think one should perform a behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: appraisal of obligation to act
bcio_terms.definition: An <emotional-relevance appraisal> that represents an evaluation of how much one is personally obliged to respond to an event or person in need.

bcio_terms.label: belief about the positive health consequences of a behaviour
bcio_terms.definition: A <belief about health consequences of behaviour> in which the consequences are evaluated as positive.

bcio_terms.label: agenda-sharing communication
bcio_terms.definition: A communication in which the initiator and one or more activity participants work together to determine a focus for the activity to be undertaken.

bcio_terms.label: health-promoting behaviour
bcio_terms.definition: A health-related behaviour that improves the person’s health.

bcio_terms.label: high frequency of past behaviour
bcio_terms.definition: A frequency of past behaviour that is above a threshold.

bcio_terms.label: behaviour starting point
bcio_terms.definition: A time point that is the start of an individual human behaviour.

bcio_terms.label: physical behavioural opportunity
bcio_terms.definition: A <behavioural opportunity> that involves time and parts of the environmental system that do not involve people or organisations.

bcio_terms.label: behaviour change intervention physical setting
bcio_terms.definition: A physical environment in which a BCI is delivered.

bcio_terms.label: face to face mode of delivery
bcio_terms.definition: Human interactional mode of delivery that involves an intervention source and recipient being together in the same location and communicating directly.

bcio_terms.label: electronic mode of delivery
bcio_terms.definition: Informational mode of delivery that involves electronic technology in the presentation of information to an intervention recipient.

bcio_terms.label: arrange support BCT
bcio_terms.definition: A <social support BCT> that organises support from another for the person.

bcio_terms.label: restructure the social environment BCT
bcio_terms.definition: A <restructure the environment BCT> that alters the social environment in which the behaviour is, or would have been, performed in a way that facilitates or impedes the behaviour.

theories.label: Diffusion of Innovations
theories.gap_pattern: Diffusion of Innovations absent: text does not report adopter categories or S-curve trajectory. Early majority vs laggard penetration uncharacterised. Reach plateaus after early adopters invisible.

theories.label: Threshold Model
theories.gap_pattern: Threshold Model absent: text does not report individual activation thresholds for adoption. Critical mass dynamics and cascade tipping point undetected. Population-level reach non-linear dynamics invisible.

theories.label: RE-AIM Framework
theories.gap_pattern: RE-AIM absent: text does not report representativeness of reached sample. Efficacy-effectiveness gap due to biased reach undetectable. Public health impact of intervention underestimated.

theories.label: Social-Ecological Model
theories.gap_pattern: Social-Ecological Model absent: text does not report multilevel reach barriers (individual, interpersonal, community, policy). Single-level reach intervention cannot address multilevel access inequities.

theories.label: Equity Theory
theories.gap_pattern: Equity Theory absent: text does not report distributional fairness of reach across population subgroups. Systematic under-reach of disadvantaged groups undetected. Intervention may widen health inequities.

theories.label: CBPR
theories.gap_pattern: CBPR absent: text does not report community participation in intervention design or delivery. Community trust deficit as reach barrier undetected. Hard-to-reach populations remain unreached.

theories.label: Tipping Point Theory
theories.gap_pattern: Tipping Point absent: text does not report whether critical mass threshold for population-level behaviour change was reached. Below-threshold intervention produces no durable collective change.

theories.label: Social Capital Theory
theories.author: Bourdieu/Putnam
theories.gap_pattern: Social Capital Theory absent: network trust and reciprocity as behaviour change resource invisible. Low social capital community requires different reach strategy from high social capital community.

theories.label: Social Exchange Theory
theories.author: Homans/Blau
theories.gap_pattern: Social Exchange Theory absent: reciprocity norms and cost-benefit calculus of behaviour change participation invisible. Exit from programme when perceived costs exceed benefits unpredicted.

theories.label: Matthew Effect
theories.author: Merton
theories.gap_pattern: Matthew Effect absent: cumulative advantage compounding over time — early reach advantage growing invisible. Intervention reinforcing existing inequalities through preferential reach.

Equity.original_question: How far will it, or is it likely to, INCREASE OR DECREASE differences between advantaged and disadvantaged sectors of society?
Equity.critical_note: Equity can move in BOTH directions. Intervention may not only reduce but also INCREASE inequality if it disproportionately benefits privileged groups. Tudor Hart's Inverse Care Law: availability of care is inversely proportional to need.

formulas_L99.weighted_gap: Eq_weighted = Σ |Reach_i - Reach_mean| × weight_i where weight_i = subpopulation share in target group
formulas_L99.slope_index_of_inequality: Reach = alpha + beta × SES_rank; beta = SII (negative beta = lower SES → lower Reach)
formulas_L99.burden_reach_mismatch: Mismatch_i = Burden_share_i - Reach_share_i (positive = bears more burden than receives coverage)
formulas_L99.concentration_index: CI = 2 × cov(Reach_i, SES_rank_i) / mean(Reach); CI=-1: all resources to poor; CI=0: equality; CI=+1: all to rich
formulas_L99.direction_check: Delta_inequality = Eq_gap_after - Eq_gap_before (negative = intervention reduced inequality; positive = increased)

source: Michie S et al. 2014 + full /L99 operationalisation

two_roles.pre_factum: Filter before launch — structured judgement at 3 horizons. Output: develop / pilot / reject.
two_roles.post_factum: Gap analysis of published studies — 5-step blind scoring protocol. Output: methodological gap verdict.

three_horizons.H1_entry_point: Should we develop this? Data: forecasts (3 scenarios). Output: develop / pilot / reject.
three_horizons.H2_instrument_selection: Which BCW combination passes APEASE? Data: forecasts + analogues. Output: ranked list.
three_horizons.H3_iterative_optimisation: What failed? What to redesign? Data: actual wave data. Output: targeted redesign.

score_interpretation.0.65_0.79: Moderate — implement with monitoring of weak dimensions
score_interpretation.0.50_0.64: Requires optimisation — one more redesign wave

trigger_conditions.E1: Input contains Problem + Goal + Result without existing BPMS/DREFS analysis
trigger_conditions.E2: Input contains existing BPMS+DREFS configuration OR wave failure data
trigger_conditions.E3: Input is a published study for retrospective APEASE gap analysis
trigger_conditions.not_triggered_by: Standard BCIO ontology queries, T1-T16 module analysis, graph mode

scope.what_this_file_answers: When does it make sense to activate APEASE? What are the 6 criteria and their BCIO ID mappings?
scope.what_full_spec_answers: How to perform deep APEASE analysis once mode is active?
scope.apease_does_not_override: BCIO primary route (T1-T16, graph mode, R1-R6). APEASE is a separate mode or post-primary evaluation.
